n: Cafe de l'Ambre (カフェ・ド・ランブル)
hook: A 'Coffee Only' temple in Ginza, run from 1948 by the man called Japan's God of Coffee.
facts: Opened in 1948 in Ginza 8-chome by Ichiro Sekiguchi, who roasted his own beans in-house.
Specialises in aged beans sourced worldwide and serves more than 30 coffee varieties.
Sekiguchi was nicknamed the 'God of Coffee' and worked at the cafe into old age, dying in 2018 at age 103.
The sign over the door reads 'Coffee Only'; the dark wood-panelled interior is essentially unchanged.
Address: 8-10-15 Ginza, Chuo-ku, near Shimbashi Station.

sources.note: History: 1948 founding, Sekiguchi, 'Coffee Only', aged beans

w: 'Coffee Only,' says the door — and that is the whole creed of this dark, wood-panelled Ginza kissaten, open since 1948. Ichiro Sekiguchi, the man they called the God of Coffee, roasted his own aged beans here and worked the counter until he died at 103. Thirty-odd brews, no food, no fuss. The pour-over as devotion.

hook: Basement jazz kissa off Yasukuni-dori that Murakami immortalised in Norwegian Wood.
facts: Opened in 1961; one of Tokyo's most famous jazz kissa (jazz coffee/listening bars).
Appears by name in Haruki Murakami's novel Norwegian Wood, where the narrator meets Midori for vodka tonics.
Founded by photographer Hozumi Nakadaira, whose photos of jazz musicians line the walls.
Sits in a basement on Yasukuni-dori near Shinjuku Station's east side and Isetan.
Music plays on a high-end audio system surrounded by vinyl; its stricter sister bar DIG banned conversation.
legend: Often cited as the place fans go to drink at the spot from Norwegian Wood, ordering Midori's vodka tonics.

sources.note: 1961 opening, Nakadaira, Norwegian Wood, Yasukuni-dori basement

w: A basement jazz kissa off Yasukuni-dori, spinning vinyl on a serious rig since 1961 — and the bar where Murakami's narrator drinks vodka tonics with Midori in Norwegian Wood. Photographer Hozumi Nakadaira founded it; his shots of jazz greats line the walls. Its sterner sister bar once banned talk. Order Midori's drink and listen.

n: Disk Union Shinjuku (ディスクユニオン新宿)
hook: Multi-floor flagship of Tokyo's legendary vinyl chain, record-store heaven.
facts: Disk Union was founded in 1967 and grew into a network of 40-plus stores across Tokyo (and one in Osaka).
Shinjuku has nineteen Disk Union shops alone, several genre-specialised (classical, latin, metal, soul/R&B, punk, etc.).
The main Shinjuku second-hand shop spans about eight floors of used vinyl and CDs.
Disk Union is parent company of DIW Records, a label specialising in avant-garde jazz.
Located on/near Shinjuku Central Street in Shinjuku 3-chome.
w: Record-store heaven, eight floors of second-hand vinyl and CDs — and that is just the main shop. Nineteen branches in Shinjuku alone, split by genre: classical, soul, metal, Latin, punk. Founded in 1967, it even spun off its own avant-garde jazz label. Lose an afternoon, floor by floor. Tokyo's temple of the crate.

hook: Cheap, garlicky, queue-out-the-door gyoza specialist a step from Omotesando.
facts: A no-frills gyoza-only specialist in Harajuku where lines of a few minutes to an hour are routine.
Serves just two styles, pan-fried (yaki) and boiled (sui) gyoza, with raw-garlic and chilli condiments on the table.
Long a budget cult favourite among locals and visitors, priced around 290-300 yen per plate.
Address: 6-2-4 Jingumae, Shibuya-ku, near Meiji-jingumae / Omotesando.

sources.note: Queue reputation, gyoza-only menu, Harajuku location

w: One thing, done relentlessly well: gyoza, fried or boiled, about 300 yen a plate, a step from the Omotesando crowds. No menu to speak of, raw garlic and chilli on the table, and a queue out the door that locals happily join. Cheap, garlicky, cult-loved. Order both styles and pile on the garlic.

hook: A tea house on stilts over the tidal pond of a shogun's bayside garden, serving whisked matcha.
facts: Sits on a tidal saltwater pond inside Hama-rikyu Gardens, reached by wooden bridges.
Hama-rikyu was built from 1654 by Tokugawa Tsunashige on land reclaimed from Edo Bay; later a shogunal villa and imperial garden.
Visitors are served freshly whisked matcha with seasonal wagashi (Japanese confectionery).
Hama-rikyu is one of only two Tokyo gardens designated both a Special Historic Site and Special Place of Scenic Beauty.
The garden opened to the public in 1946 after transfer from the Imperial Household to Tokyo.

sources.note: Teahouse on the tidal pond, matcha with wagashi

w: A tea house on stilts over a tidal saltwater pond, reached by wooden bridges, in the shogun's old bayside garden Hama-rikyu. They whisk you a bowl of matcha with a seasonal sweet while the water rises and falls with Tokyo Bay around you. The garden was laid out from 1654 for a Tokugawa lord. Tea, with the tide coming in.

hook: Tokyo's oldest surviving daimyo garden, an Edo strolling landscape in miniature.
facts: Construction began in 1629 under Tokugawa Yorifusa, first lord of the Mito branch; completed and named by his successor Tokugawa Mitsukuni.
It is the oldest surviving daimyo (feudal-lord) garden in Tokyo.
Named 'Korakuen' from a Chinese poem urging a ruler to enjoy pleasure only after securing his people's happiness.
The strolling layout centres on the Dai-sensui pond (mimicking Lake Biwa) with the stone Engetsu-kyo 'Round Moon Bridge'.
Designated both a Special Historic Site and Special Place of Scenic Beauty (elevated to special status in 1952).

sources.note: Oldest daimyo garden, 1629, Mito Tokugawa, naming

w: Tokyo's oldest daimyo garden, begun in 1629 for the Mito branch of the Tokugawa — a strolling landscape that miniaturises famous views, with a pond standing in for Lake Biwa and a stone round-moon bridge mirrored in the water. The name urges a ruler to take his pleasure only after his people's. Edo, kept green among the towers.

hook: Smoky postwar 'Memory Lane' of cramped yakitori stalls beside Shinjuku Station, aka Piss Alley.
facts: A warren of tiny yakitori, motsuyaki and noodle stalls just outside Shinjuku Station's west exit (Nishi-Shinjuku).
Its alleys date to the 1940s, when post-war black-market traders sold food and scarce goods around the station.
Nicknamed Shonben Yokocho ('Piss Alley') because for years there were no toilets and drunk revellers relieved themselves in the lane.
A 1999 fire destroyed much of the area, after which it was rebuilt.
Today it is a popular after-work spot for cheap food and drink, the standing/seated counters seating only a handful each.
legend: The 'Piss Alley' nickname commemorates the toilet-less, hard-drinking post-war years.

sources.note: 1940s origins, black market, Piss Alley name, 1999 fire

w: 'Memory Lane' — a warren of tiny smoke-blackened yakitori stalls jammed against Shinjuku Station's west side, each counter seating a handful. The alleys grew out of the 1940s black market; for years there were no toilets, which earned it the other name, Piss Alley. Skewers, beer, elbow room only. Squeeze in where you can.

n: Meguro Parasitological Museum (目黒寄生虫館)
hook: A free two-floor museum dedicated entirely to parasites, home to an 8.8-metre tapeworm.
facts: Founded in 1953 by physician Dr. Satoru Kamegai; admission is free.
The only museum in the world devoted solely to parasites.
Holds about 60,000 specimens, with roughly 300 on public display.
Star exhibit is an 8.8-metre tapeworm once extracted from a human host.
Located in a quiet residential part of Meguro, near Meguro Station.
legend: A cult date-spot reputation: visitors buy tapeworm-themed merchandise in the ground-floor shop.

sources.note: 1953 founding, Kamegai, only parasite museum, 8.8m tapeworm

w: The only museum on earth devoted entirely to parasites — two quiet floors in residential Meguro, free to enter, 60,000 specimens, and a star attraction: an 8.8-metre tapeworm pulled from a single human host. Dr Satoru Kamegai founded it in 1953. The gift shop sells tapeworm souvenirs, and yes, it's a cult date spot. Squirm and learn.
